label: PLAN Frigate 532 Dangerous Maneuver vs. BRP Benguet — Mar 25, 2026
notes: PLAN missile frigate 532 made an 'unsafe and unprofessional maneuver' closing in at dangerous distance against BRP Benguet (LS-507) near Pag-asa Island. AFP WESCOM called it 'intentional.' Col. Nep Padua cited COLREGs violations. China counter-claimed (March 28) that BRP Benguet 'ignored radio warnings.' Third PLAN warship incident in the Spratlys in three weeks (after March 7 fire-control radar lock on BRP Miguel Malvar at Sabina Shoal).

label: Resupply Mission — Aug 5, 2023
notes: Unaizah May 1 got through; Unaizah May 2 repelled by CCG water cannons. BRP Malabrigo and BRP Cabra escorted. China deployed ~14 vessels to intercept.

label: Resupply Mission — Dec 10, 2023
notes: 46 Chinese ships vs 4 Philippine vessels. Water cannons disabled one ship's engines. CCG collided with supply ship carrying AFP Chief of Staff. Major escalation; both sides accused the other of ramming.

label: Resupply Mission — Feb 4, 2024
notes: China fired water cannons at BRP Cabra and supply boats. Navigation bridge damaged; crew injuries. China called the Philippine approach 'deliberate provocation.' US condemned the use of water cannons.

label: Unaizah Mae 4 Attack — Mar 23, 2024
notes: CCG water cannon barrages destroyed Unaizah Mae 4 superstructure and disabled propulsion. BRP Sindangan towed stricken vessel back. Four Philippine Navy sailors injured. Philippines filed diplomatic protest.

label: Resupply Mission — Jun 17, 2024 (Most Serious)
notes: China deployed 8 small craft; CCG personnel boarded Philippine RHIBs with bladed weapons. 8 sailors injured (1 lost thumb); M16 rifle, ammo, food and supplies seized. Only second time China succeeded in blocking resupply since 2021. US condemned; MDT invocations followed.
damage: 2 Philippine RHIBs destroyed; M16 + ammo + supplies seized

label: Resupply Mission — Jul 2024 (Provisional Arrangement)
notes: Following June 17 incident, Philippines and China announced provisional resupply arrangement in July 2024. Arrangement broke down within weeks as China resumed water cannon use claiming Philippines attempted to transport construction materials.

label: Resupply Mission — Aug 2024 (Arrangement Collapse)
notes: Multiple water cannon incidents followed collapse of provisional arrangement. Philippine RHIBs damaged. Gen. Brawner confirmed personnel injuries. Both sides blamed each other for violating the July 2024 understanding.

label: Pag-asa Island Supply Route
notes: Supply route from El Nido, Palawan to Pag-asa (Thitu Island), Philippines' largest occupied Spratly feature. Chinese maritime militia vessels maintain persistent presence around Thitu Island, surveilling Philippine supply missions.

label: CCG Patrol: Fiery Cross → Second Thomas Shoal
notes: Routine China Coast Guard patrol route from Fiery Cross Reef command hub to blockade position near Second Thomas Shoal. Average transit ~3 hours at full speed. CCG deployed 14+ vessels per intercept mission by 2023.

label: CCG Patrol: Mischief Reef → Second Thomas
notes: Short patrol route from Mischief Reef (largest Spratly outpost) to Second Thomas Shoal intercept position. Mischief Reef is the closest major Chinese outpost to Second Thomas Shoal. CCG vessels stage from here for immediate intercept operations.

label: CCG Patrol Route: Fiery Cross → Scarborough
notes: China Coast Guard patrol route connecting Fiery Cross Reef command hub to Scarborough Shoal enforcement zone. CCG maintains continuous presence at Scarborough Shoal entry point, blocking Philippine fishermen. Water cannon incidents in December 2023, April 2024, October 2024, December 2024.

label: China Militia: Subi Reef → Pag-asa Harassment
notes: Chinese maritime militia vessels routinely deploy from Subi Reef (16 nm from Pag-asa) to circle and surveil Philippines' largest Spratly outpost. CSIS AMTI documented militia at Pag-asa 'in greater numbers and on a more constant basis than ever before' since 2021.

label: China Militia: Fiery Cross → Whitsun Reef (Mar 2021)
notes: ~220 Chinese maritime militia vessels assembled at Whitsun Reef, first sighted December 2020, reported publicly March 7, 2021. Organized and staged from Fiery Cross, Subi, and Mischief Reefs. By March 31, vessels dispersed to McKennan/Hughes Reefs and near Pag-asa Island.

label: PLA Navy: Hainan → Spratly Islands Patrol
notes: Routine PLAN surface combatant and submarine transit from Yulin Naval Base (Hainan) to Spratly Islands operating area. PLAN increased patrols significantly after 2020. Type 055 destroyers, Type 052D destroyers, and Type 075 LHDs deployed to South Sea Fleet for SCS operations.

label: PLA Paracel Military Exercises — Jul 2020
notes: China conducted military exercises around the Paracel Islands July 1–5, 2020. US called the drills a violation of 2002 Declaration on Conduct. US responded by deploying USS Nimitz and USS Ronald Reagan carrier strike groups for first dual-carrier SCS exercises since 2014.

label: PLA Taiwan Encirclement — Aug 2022 (Post-Pelosi)
notes: Largest PLA exercises since 1996 Third Taiwan Strait Crisis, triggered by Speaker Pelosi's Taiwan visit. Live-fire, ballistic missiles over Taiwan, nominal blockade simulation. ~450 PLA aircraft entered Taiwan ADIZ in August 2022—highest monthly total ever. Before Pelosi: 23 median line crossings total; after: 563 incursions in 2022 alone.

label: 'Joint Sword' Exercises — Apr 8-10, 2023
notes: PLA Eastern Theater Command 'all-round encirclement' after Tsai-McCarthy meeting in California. 232 PLA aircraft near Taiwan in 3 days; 134 entered ADIZ. Su-30s and H-6 bombers. Matched Pelosi-era numbers in less than half the time.

label: 'Joint Sword-2024A' — May 23-24, 2024
notes: Response to President Lai Ching-te's inauguration. 111 aircraft and 46 naval vessels around Taiwan; 82 crossed median line. First time China Coast Guard participated alongside PLA in joint military operations around Taiwan.

label: China Patrol: Fiery Cross → Sabina Shoal 2024
notes: China deployed up to 40 ships (including 3 PLAN warships) to Sabina Shoal during the 2024 standoff with BRP Teresa Magbanua. First time PLAN warships participated alongside CCG in Spratly area confrontation. BRP Teresa Magbanua rammed August 31, 2024.

label: US FONOP: Near Subi Reef
notes: US Freedom of Navigation Operation within 12 nm of Subi Reef, asserting the artificial island has no lawful territorial sea. US has conducted FONOPs in the South China Sea regularly since 2015. China's PLAN typically shadows US vessels during FONOPs and issues warnings.

label: US FONOP: Near Fiery Cross Reef
notes: US FONOP challenging excessive Chinese maritime claim within 12 nm of Fiery Cross Reef. USS Ralph Johnson (DDG-114) conducted FONOP February 2022 near Fiery Cross. China's PLAN issued 15 warnings and deployed aircraft/ships. US NavForces WEST-NAVEUR confirmed 'lawful navigation.'

label: US FONOP: Near Mischief Reef
notes: US Freedom of Navigation Operation within 12 nm of Mischief Reef, the largest Chinese artificial island and the one within Philippines' EEZ. USS Milius (DDG-69) conducted FONOP March 2023. China claimed the vessel 'illegally intruded.' US stated it conducted 'lawful freedom of navigation operations.'

label: US Dual-Carrier Exercise — Jul 4-9, 2020
notes: USS Nimitz (CVN-68) and USS Ronald Reagan (CVN-76) carrier strike groups conducted simultaneous exercises in South China Sea during China's Paracel exercises—first dual-carrier SCS drills since 2014. Explicit response to Chinese military exercises and Pompeo's July 13 rejection of Chinese SCS claims.

label: US Carrier Transit: Luzon Strait → SCS
notes: Routine US Navy carrier strike group transit through the Luzon Strait into the South China Sea. USS Carl Vinson primary SCS carrier 2024–2025. US carriers operate from the South China Sea to demonstrate freedom of navigation and deter Chinese aggression against the Philippines.

label: US P-8A Poseidon Maritime Patrol Route
notes: Routine P-8A Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft route from Andersen AFB Guam through the South China Sea. China has intercepted P-8s multiple times: PLA J-11 released chaff in P-8 flight path (2021); J-16 performed 'unnecessarily aggressive maneuver' causing P-8 to fly through turbulence (2023). US continues patrols regardless.

label: US-Philippines Joint Patrol: Scarborough Area
notes: US-Philippines joint maritime patrols near Scarborough Shoal, resumed under Marcos administration. US Coast Guard vessels joined Philippine Coast Guard patrols. China protested joint patrols as 'provocative.' US cited them as demonstration of treaty alliance obligations.

label: US-Philippines Joint Patrol: Second Thomas
notes: US Navy vessels conducted joint patrols with Philippine Coast Guard near Second Thomas Shoal following escalation of Chinese harassment. Task Force Ayungin (revealed November 2024 by Secretary Austin) is the dedicated US unit supporting Philippine forces at Second Thomas Shoal.

label: NMESIS Anti-Ship Coverage: Luzon Strait
notes: 3rd Marine Littoral Regiment's NMESIS launchers positioned at Batanes Islands during Balikatan 2025 cover the Luzon Strait with ~100 nm Naval Strike Missile range. Any Chinese surface vessel transiting from Pacific to South China Sea through Luzon Strait falls within engagement range. First overseas NMESIS deployment.

label: Balikatan 2024 — First Exercise Outside Territorial Waters
notes: Balikatan 2024 exercise area extended for first time beyond Philippines' 12 nm territorial waters, directly challenging Chinese EEZ claims. France participated as full partner. Live-fire missile test sank target vessel. ~16,000 US, Philippine, Australian, French troops.

label: US FONOP: Near Scarborough Shoal
notes: US vessels transited near Scarborough Shoal during Balikatan 2024 exercises, coinciding with a Chinese water cannon attack on Philippine vessels (April 30, 2024). US State Department condemned China's actions at Scarborough as 'dangerous and provocative.' US Freedom of Navigation asserts Chinese presence does not constitute lawful territorial waters.

label: AUKUS Submarine Transit: Lombok Strait → SCS
notes: Planned transit route for future AUKUS nuclear-powered submarines from HMAS Stirling (Western Australia) through Lombok Strait into South China Sea. Nuclear-powered submarines transit Lombok Strait to avoid shallow Malacca Strait. SRF-West at HMAS Stirling to begin in 2027 with up to 4 US Virginia-class submarines.

label: AUKUS Partner Route: Singapore → SCS
notes: Route for US Littoral Combat Ships rotating from Changi Naval Base, Singapore into South China Sea patrol areas. UK Royal Navy and Australian warships also transit this route. Singapore-based US vessels provide sustained presence monitoring Spratly Islands and EEZ intrusions.

label: Strategic Transit: Malacca Strait → SCS
notes: Main shipping lane through Malacca Strait to South China Sea. 85,000+ vessel transits per year; 30%+ of global trade. China's 'Malacca Dilemma'—dependence on this US Navy-secured route is a strategic vulnerability. A South China Sea conflict could threaten this $5.3T annual trade corridor.

label: Japan-Philippines Joint Maritime Patrol
notes: Following signing of Japan-Philippines Reciprocal Access Agreement (July 8, 2024), Japan MSDF and Philippine Navy conduct joint maritime patrols in the South China Sea. Philippines is the first Asian nation to sign such a pact with Japan. RAA entered into force September 2025.

label: Japan MSDF South China Sea Transit
notes: Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force vessels transit the South China Sea increasingly as Japan deepens Indo-Pacific engagement. Japan has conducted freedom of navigation operations and intelligence-gathering missions. Kishida: 'Ukraine today may be East Asia tomorrow.' Japan hosted first trilateral US-Japan-Philippines summit April 2024.

label: CCG Floating Barrier — Scarborough Shoal 2023
notes: China deployed a floating barrier (water boom) at the southeast entrance to Scarborough Shoal in September 2023. Philippines physically removed it. Another floating barrier deployed December 2023 with simultaneous water cannon attacks on 3 Philippine vessels. China also placed barriers at BRP Sierra Madre approach area October 2023.
damage: Barrier used to block Philippine access to traditional fishing grounds

label: DF-26B/DF-21D Test Fires — Aug 26, 2020
notes: China fired anti-ship ballistic missiles (DF-21D 'carrier killer' and DF-26B 'Guam killer') into the South China Sea on August 26, 2020 during PLA exercises. Trajectory approximately from inland China to South China Sea impact zone. US confirmed the launches. China said they demonstrated ability to strike 'moving targets at sea.'

label: US-Philippines Patrol: Second Thomas Area 2023
notes: US and Philippine vessels conducted joint maritime patrols south of Palawan toward Second Thomas Shoal corridor. Marcos administration reinstated and expanded joint patrols after Duterte era freeze. US Coast Guard and Navy vessels participated alongside Philippine Navy and PCG.

label: PLAN Transit: Hainan → Pacific (Luzon Strait)
notes: Routine PLAN naval transit from Yulin Naval Base (Hainan) through the Luzon Strait into the Philippine Sea / Western Pacific. China has increased frequency of such transits, practicing 'island chain breakout' scenarios. US monitors all PLAN Pacific transits via P-8 and submarine surveillance.

label: France Joins Balikatan 2024 as Full Partner
notes: France participated as a full partner (not observer) in Balikatan 2024 for the first time, marking Europe's direct military engagement in South China Sea defense. French warship transited from Singapore to Philippine waters for the exercise. France's engagement reflects European concern over Indo-Pacific security and free navigation.

label: PLA Combat Readiness Patrol: Hainan → Scarborough — Mar 30, 2026
notes: PLA Southern Theater Command published video of combat readiness patrols near Scarborough Shoal on March 30, featuring two Type 054A guided-missile frigates and J-15 carrier-based fighters. PLA described mission as 'tracking, monitoring, warnings, and expulsions' — a direct response to Philippine 'provocations' following the BRP Benguet near-collision (March 25). Escalatory deployment of carrier-based aviation alongside surface combatants in the South China Sea.

label: CCG Cutters: Scarborough → Batanes Islands (Bashi Channel) — Apr 2026
notes: Three Chinese coast guard cutters (each 330+ ft) tracked 'bracketing' the Philippines' northernmost Batanes Islands near the Bashi Channel — the strategic waterway between Luzon and Taiwan. Described by analyst Ray Powell as 'a new level of aggression.' Batanes is closer to Taiwan than to Manila. Coincides with Balikatan 2026 preparations and NMESIS deployments to Batanes area.

label: Japan JSDF Combat Deployment: Balikatan 2026
notes: Japan will send combat-capable Self-Defense Forces units to Balikatan 2026 (starting ~April 20) for the first time since WWII, with troops carrying weapons during joint exercises. Exercise focuses on coastal defense, anti-ship tactics, and area denial. Australia and France also participating. AFP Chief declared it 'biggest ever' despite US-Iran conflict.

label: CCG Floating Barrier Deployment: Scarborough Shoal — Apr 10-11, 2026
notes: China installed a 352-meter floating barrier at the entrance channel to Scarborough Shoal between April 10-11, 2026, using 4 Chinese fishing vessels and a CCG patrol vessel. Part of a wider pattern of SCS access restriction documented by FDD (April 16, 2026). ~10 CCG vessels maintained above-average presence near Scarborough April 5-12. Third deployment of a floating barrier at Scarborough (after Sept 2023 and Dec 2023 incidents). Philippine officials condemned the barrier but did not announce immediate removal plans.

label: Japan JSDF Naval Transit: Okinawa → Balikatan 2026 Exercise Zone
notes: Japan Self-Defense Forces deploying 1,400 troops plus naval vessels from Okinawa/Kyushu area south to Balikatan 2026 exercise staging areas in northern Luzon (April 20–May 8, 2026). Japan's contribution includes Type 88 Surface-to-Ship Missile systems — the first time Japan has deployed anti-ship missiles capable of denying Chinese naval access to the South China Sea in a formal Philippine exercise. This marks Japan's most direct military engagement in SCS defense exercises since WWII.

label: US-Australia-Philippines Trilateral MCA — Apr 9-12, 2026
notes: Four-day multilateral Maritime Cooperative Activity (MCA) conducted April 9-12 by US, Australian, and Philippine naval forces within the Philippine EEZ. Included warships, fighter jets, and surveillance aircraft. Second such trilateral MCA of 2026; immediately preceded by PLA Southern Theater Command 'tracking and expulsion' statement on April 13 accusing Philippines of 'rallying extra-regional countries.'

label: Japan Type 88 SINKEX — BRP Quezon, Balikatan 2026
notes: Japan Ground Self-Defense Force Type 88 Surface-to-Ship Missile (SSM-1) live-fire exercise against decommissioned Philippine Navy vessel BRP Quezon (PS-70) during Balikatan 2026 (April 20–May 8, 2026). First time Japan has fired anti-ship missiles in a combined exercise in the Philippines since WWII. Trilateral coordination between JSDF, AFP, and US forces. Type 88 has ~150 km range with active radar homing. Japan's Defense Minister Koizumi observed from Manila. Exercise demonstrates US-Japan-Philippines sea-denial capability in the South China Sea approach and Luzon Strait corridor.
